title: Teaching AI to Want Good Things

modules.title: Why Alignment Is an Urgent Problem
modules.sub: Framing the stakes before AI systems grow more powerful
modules.description: Learners examine why the gap between what AI systems do and what humans intend them to do matters so much right now. They weigh historical analogies and current capability trends to build a personal sense of urgency and context for the rest of the course.

modules.title: How AI Systems Develop Unexpected Goals
modules.sub: Tracing the origins of misaligned behavior in trained models
modules.description: Learners explore how optimization processes can produce systems that pursue proxy objectives rather than intended ones. They analyze concrete case studies to recognize the patterns that lead to goal misgeneralization and emergent misalignment.

modules.title: Reward Hacking: When AI Games the Rules
modules.sub: Understanding how AI exploits loopholes in its own objectives
modules.description: Learners investigate documented examples where AI agents found unintended shortcuts to maximize their reward signals. They assess why specifying a reward function that captures human intent is far harder than it first appears.

modules.title: Human Feedback and Its Limitations
modules.sub: Evaluating reinforcement learning from human feedback critically
modules.description: Learners examine how RLHF shapes AI behavior and why it has become a dominant alignment technique. They then critically assess the failure modes — including evaluator bias, manipulation, and scalability — that prevent it from being a complete solution.

modules.title: Interpretability: Understanding AI From Inside
modules.sub: Looking inside neural networks to verify what they have learned
modules.description: Learners survey the growing field of mechanistic interpretability and the tools researchers use to understand what is happening inside large models. They evaluate what interpretability can and cannot yet tell us about whether an AI system is trustworthy.

modules.title: Governance and Global Safety Efforts
modules.sub: Mapping the institutions and policies shaping AI safety outcomes
modules.description: Learners review the landscape of national and international efforts to govern advanced AI, from regulatory frameworks to voluntary industry commitments. They assess the coordination challenges that make global AI governance uniquely difficult and identify where progress is being made.

modules.title: Catastrophic Risk: Real Scenarios Explained
modules.sub: Grounding worst-case AI risk scenarios in concrete reasoning
modules.description: Learners work through specific catastrophic risk scenarios — including misuse, misalignment, and structural power concentration — without relying on science fiction framing. They practice distinguishing well-supported risk arguments from speculative ones so they can engage these debates credibly.

modules.title: What Individuals Can Do About AI Safety
modules.sub: Translating alignment awareness into meaningful personal action
modules.description: Learners identify concrete ways professionals outside AI research can contribute to safer AI development, from career pivots to organizational advocacy to informed citizenship. They leave the course with a personal action plan tailored to their own role, skills, and sphere of influence.

synopsis: AI safety and alignment asks one of the most important questions of our time: how do we build powerful AI systems that reliably pursue goals humans actually care about? This course demystifies the technical and philosophical challenges of alignment for a general audience, explaining real risks and real solutions without requiring a computer science degree.
rationale: Despite a corpus similarity of 0.65 to an existing single discussion prompt on alignment, the demand score of 6/10 and the depth of public discourse around AI safety indicate a strong need for a structured, multi-module course that builds genuine understanding of this field.
nearest_existing: If we build systems smarter than us, how do we ensure they want what we want?
